items.title: Peptic ulcer as the main cause
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause: peptic ulcer

  • Duodenal peptic ulcer is the most common cause of UGIB (40-50% of cases), followed by gastric ulcer.
  • The pathophysiology involves an imbalance between aggressive factors (acid, H. pylori, NSAIDs) and defensive factors (mucus, bicarbonate, prostaglandins).
  • Bleeding occurs due to erosion of a submucosal arterial vessel; The risk of rebleeding is related to the size of the ulcer (>2 cm) and the location on the posterior surface of the duodenal bulb or lesser gastric curvature.
  • DDx: always rule out esophageal varices in patients with liver disease, and Mallory-Weiss if there is a history of repeated vomiting.

items.title: Clinical and therapeutic classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification: variceal vs non-variceal

  • AGIB is divided into variceal (20% of cases) and non-variceal (80%). The first is associated with portal hypertension (cirrhosis, portal thrombosis).
  • Variceal UGIB has higher mortality (15-20%) and early rebleeding, requiring management with vasoactive agents (terlipressin, somatostatin) and endoscopic ligation.
  • Non-variceal UGIB includes peptic ulcer, Mallory-Weiss, erosive gastritis, esophagitis, neoplasms; initial management is with PPI and endoscopy.
  • Trap: in a patient with cirrhosis and UGIB, do not automatically assume variceal origin; Up to 50% may have non-variceal bleeding (ulcer, portal gastropathy).

items.title: Rockall scale
items.contentHtml:
Rockall scale for prognosis

  • The Rockall scale predicts mortality after UGIB; It is calculated with age, shock, comorbidities, endoscopic diagnosis and signs of active bleeding.
  • Score: age (<60=0, 60-79=1, >79=2), shock (systolic BP >100 without tachycardia=0, >100 with HR >100=1, <100=2), comorbidities (none=0, heart/kidney/liver failure=2, disseminated neoplasia=3).
  • Endoscopic score: Mallory-Weiss/no lesion=0, other lesions=1, neoplasia=2; active bleeding/adherent clot=2.
  • Threshold: Rockall ≥5 indicates high risk of mortality; Rockall 0-2 has mortality <0.5%.

items.title: Score Glasgow-Blatchford
items.contentHtml:
Glasgow-Blatchford Score for risk

  • The Glasgow-Blatchford score (GBS) evaluates the need for intervention (transfusion, endoscopy, surgery) in UGIB, without requiring initial endoscopy.
  • Variables: urea (>6.5 mmol/L=2, >8=3, >10=4, >25=6), Hb (man <120 g/L=3, <100=6; woman <100=1), systolic BP <100=3, HR >100=1, melena=1, syncope=2, liver disease=2, insuf. cardiac=2.
  • Threshold: GBS=0 allows safe outpatient management (intervention risk <2%). GBS ≥12 indicates high risk of death or rebleeding.
  • Catch: GBS does not require endoscopy, unlike Rockall; useful in triage.

items.title: Endoscopic timing
items.contentHtml:
Endoscopy within 24 hours

  • Endoscopy within 24 hours is indicated in all stable UGIB, with greater benefit if performed in the first 12 hours in high-risk patients (GBS ≥12, Rockall ≥5).
  • In UGIB with hemodynamic instability (systolic BP <100, HR >100, Hb <7 g/dL) prior resuscitation and urgent endoscopy (<6 hours) are required if suspicion of active bleeding persists.
  • Key finding: early endoscopy (<12h) reduces hospital stay and need for transfusion, but does not reduce mortality if the patient is stable.
  • Trap: in variceal UGIB, the endoscopy should be performed as soon as possible after stabilization; delay >12 hours increases mortality.

items.title: Combined endoscopic therapy
items.contentHtml:
Endoscopic therapy: adrenaline + cautery

  • Combined endoscopic therapy (1:10,000 adrenaline injection + thermal or mechanical cautery) is the standard for ulcers with active bleeding or visible non-bleeding vessel.
  • adrenaline produces vasoconstriction and hydrostatic tamponade; 0.5-2 mL are injected in 4 quadrants around the glass (total dose <20 mL).
  • The cautery (bipolar or heater probe) applies heat to coagulate the glass; used in combination to reduce rebleeding (rate <15%).
  • Alternative: endoscopic clips (hemoclips) are equally effective; in gastric ulcers that are difficult to access, cautery is preferred.

items.title: Band ligation
items.contentHtml:
Band ligation in esophageal varices

  • band ligation (EVL) is the endoscopic therapy of choice for bleeding esophageal varices, with hemostasis rates >90%.
  • Elastic bands are applied to the neck of the varices; It is contraindicated in gastric varices (prefer cyanoacrylate).
  • Prophylaxis: after hemostasis, start propranolol or carvedilol (dose titrated to reduce heart rate by 25%) and repeat EVL every 2-4 weeks until eradication.
  • Trap: ligation should not be performed if the patient has bleeding. massive asset without display; In that case, use the Sengstaken-Blakemore ball as a bridge.

items.title: PPI in HDA
items.contentHtml:
Use of proton pump inhibitors

  • In non-variceal UGIB, PPI (omeprazole 80 mg IV bolus, then 8 mg/h in continuous infusion) reduces rebleeding and the need for surgery after endoscopic therapy.
  • Continuous infusion is maintained for 72 hours in high-risk ulcers (Forrest Ia, Ib, IIa, IIb).
  • In variceal UGIB, the PPI is not routinely indicated; only if concomitant ulcer is suspected or after ligation (to prevent post-ligation ulcers).
  • Threshold: maintain gastric pH >6 to stabilize the clot; the 80 mg bolus followed by infusion achieves this goal.
  • Trap: in active bleeding, do not use PPI before endoscopy if endoscopic therapy is planned; can mask bleeding.

items.title: Antibiotics in cirrhosis
items.contentHtml:
Prophylactic antibiotics in cirrhosis

  • In all cirrhotic patients with UGIB, prophylactic antibiotic therapy (ceftriaxone 1 g IV/24h or norfloxacin 400 mg PO/12h) should be administered for 7 days to prevent spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and sepsis.
  • Prophylaxis reduces the incidence of bacterial infections of the 45% to 15% and reduces short-term mortality.
  • First line: ceftriaxone is preferred in patients with advanced cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) or in areas with high resistance to quinolones.
  • Trap: do not delay antibiotics until the results of cultures; start immediately if UGIB is suspected in cirrhosis.

items.title: Rebleeding as a predictor
items.contentHtml:
Rebleeding: predictor of mortality

  • Rebleeding is the main independent predictor of mortality in UGIB, increasing the risk from 5% to 30-40%.
  • Associated factors: ulcer >2 cm, location on the posterior surface of the duodenal bulb, shock on admission, Hb <8 g/dL, need for >4 units of transfusion.
  • In variceal UGIB, early rebleeding (<5 days) is associated with advanced liver failure (Child-Pugh C) and portal thrombosis.
  • Management: in the event of rebleeding after initial endoscopic therapy, repeat endoscopy; if unsuccessful, consider TIPS (intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) or surgery.
  • Threshold: rebleeding within the first 72 hours doubles mortality.

items.title: High-risk endoscopic signs
items.contentHtml:
High risk signs: visible vessel, clot

  • The Forrest classification defines endoscopic signs: Ia (active jet bleeding), Ib (active sheet bleeding), IIa (visible non-bleeding vessel), IIb (adhered clot), IIc (flat spot), III (clean base).
  • High risk signs are Forrest Ia, Ib, IIa and IIb; require endoscopic therapy and IV PPI.
  • Key finding: a visible vessel (Forrest IIa) has a 50% risk of rebleeding without treatment; combination therapy reduces it to 15%.
  • An adhered clot (Forrest IIb) must be removed with lavage to determine the underlying lesion; If there is visible vessel, it is treated.

items.title: HDA surgery
items.contentHtml:
Indication for surgery: endoscopic failure

  • Surgery is indicated in UGIB when endoscopic therapy fails (persistent rebleeding after two attempts) or in uncontrollable massive bleeding.
  • In peptic ulcer, the surgery of choice is gastrectomy with resection of the ulcer or ligation of the bleeding vessel (gastrotomy and suture).
  • In refractory variceal UGIB, transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic bypass (TIPS) is the alternative to surgery; shunt surgery (porto-cava) is reserved for TIPS failure.
  • Trap: do not indicate surgery if the patient has advanced cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C) without the possibility of transplant; consider TIPS or palliative management.
